metadata.scope: Plain-language first-read guidance for every CO 250 unit.

guides.bigIdea: Turn a word problem into three things: choices, a score, and rules.
guides.everydayAnalogy: Planning a grocery budget: choose amounts, decide what 'best' means, and stay inside the limits.
guides.firstCheck: Before writing equations, say what each variable means and include its units.

guides.bigIdea: Some decisions must be whole numbers or yes/no, and some relationships are curved instead of linear.
guides.everydayAnalogy: You can buy 2 buses but not 2.4 buses; turning a facility on is a yes/no decision.
guides.firstCheck: Ask which variables must be integer, binary, or continuous before choosing the model type.

guides.bigIdea: An LP can have no feasible point, improve forever, or have a finite best value—and each claim needs evidence.
guides.everydayAnalogy: A trip can be impossible, have no speed limit, or have a fastest legal route; first identify which situation you are in.
guides.firstCheck: Do not confuse an empty feasible set with an objective that improves without bound.

guides.bigIdea: Rewrite an LP into one common equation-and-nonnegativity format so simplex can read it.
guides.everydayAnalogy: Convert different plug shapes to one adapter standard before connecting them to the same machine.
guides.firstCheck: Track what happens to every inequality, free variable, and sign restriction during conversion.

guides.bigIdea: Simplex moves from one corner solution to a better neighboring corner until no improving move remains.
guides.everydayAnalogy: Climb a hill by checking nearby paths; stop when every available step goes downhill.
guides.firstCheck: Choose an improving entering variable, then use only positive entering-column entries in the ratio test.

guides.bigIdea: When no starting corner is obvious, solve a temporary problem whose only job is to find one.
guides.everydayAnalogy: Use training wheels to get moving, then remove them before the real ride.
guides.firstCheck: Phase I tests feasibility; Phase II restores the original objective.

guides.bigIdea: The algebra of bases matches the geometry of corners and tight constraints.
guides.everydayAnalogy: At a room's corner, enough walls meet to pin down your position.
guides.firstCheck: List the tight constraints first, then ask whether their normal vectors have enough independent directions.

guides.bigIdea: Every LP has a second price-based viewpoint that gives bounds on the original problem.
guides.everydayAnalogy: Value a production plan either by what it earns or by pricing the resources it consumes.
guides.firstCheck: Match each primal constraint to one dual variable and each primal variable to one dual constraint.

guides.bigIdea: At optimality, a positive variable and unused slack cannot occur on opposite sides of the same primal-dual pair.
guides.everydayAnalogy: A resource left unused should have zero scarcity price; a resource with positive price should be fully used.
guides.firstCheck: Check feasibility first, then multiply each variable by its corresponding slack.

guides.bigIdea: The LP relaxation allows fractions; valid cuts remove fractional points without removing any legal integer solution.
guides.everydayAnalogy: A calculation may suggest 2.5 boxes, but a cut records the real rule that only complete boxes count.
guides.firstCheck: For every proposed cut, verify both: all integer-feasible points satisfy it, and the target fractional point violates it.

guides.bigIdea: Convexity makes curved optimization manageable: a local certificate can prove a global best solution.
guides.everydayAnalogy: In a bowl-shaped landscape, once every allowed direction points uphill, you are at the bottom of the whole bowl.
guides.firstCheck: Before using KKT, check convexity, differentiability, feasibility, and a Slater point.
